records.ety: [AS. miscan; akin to OHG. misken, G. mischen, Russ. mieshate, W. mysgu, Gael. measg, L. miscere, mixtum, Gr. mi`sgein, migny`nai, Skr. miçra mixed. The English word has been influenced by L. miscere, mixtum (cf. Mixture), and even the AS. miscan may have been borrowed fr. L. miscere. Cf. Admix, Mash to bruise, Meddle.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Mixed (mkst) (less properly Mixt); p. pr. & vb. n. Mixing.]

defs: To cause a promiscuous interpenetration of the parts of, as of two or more substances with each other, or of one substance with others; to unite or blend into one mass or compound, as by stirring together; to mingle; to blend; as, to mix flour and salt; to mix wines.
quotes: Fair persuasions mixed with sugared words.

defs: To unite with in company; to join; to associate.
quotes: Ephraim, he hath mixed himself among the people.

defs: To form by mingling; to produce by the stirring together of ingredients; to compound of different parts.
quotes: I have chosen an argument mixed of religious and civil considerations.

defs: To combine (two or more activities) within a specified or implied time frame; as, to mix studying and partying while at college.
To become united into a compound; to be blended promiscuously together.
To associate; to mingle; as, Democrats and Republicans mixed freely at the party.
quotes: He had mixed Again in fancied safety with his kind.
